title: Drug development — how new medicines are tested
examTip: AQA loves asking about the stages of clinical trials and what double-blind means. Know thalidomide as the reason rigorous testing was introduced, and always be able to explain why a double-blind placebo-controlled trial eliminates bias.
introTips: This lesson explains why every new drug goes through years of testing before anyone is allowed to take it.
The thalidomide story is the key historical example — know it, and know why it changed everything.
The double-blind placebo trial is the most important concept — make sure you can explain exactly why it removes bias.
intro: Every tablet you've ever swallowed has a story behind it — years of tests, failures, refinements, and ethical reviews before it ever reached a pharmacy shelf. That process exists because of what happened when it didn't. Understanding drug development means understanding both the science and the hard lessons that shaped it.

keyPoints.heading: Why rigorous testing matters — the thalidomide lesson
keyPoints.content: In the 1950s and 60s, thalidomide was prescribed to pregnant women to relieve morning sickness. It hadn't been tested on pregnant animals, and the effect on a developing foetus was completely unknown. The result was over 10,000 babies born with severe limb deformities, and many more stillbirths. It was a catastrophe caused directly by inadequate testing. Thalidomide was withdrawn. The global response was to introduce much stricter drug-testing regulations — requiring drugs to be tested for safety, efficacy (whether it actually works), and dosage (the right amount) before approval. As a later note: thalidomide is now used to treat certain cancers and the inflammatory disease leprosy — but under very strict controls, never for pregnant women. The lesson isn't that drugs are dangerous; it's that untested drugs are.
writeBullets: Thalidomide: prescribed 1950s–60s to pregnant women for morning sickness
Not tested on pregnant animals → unknown effect on developing foetus
Result: 10,000+ babies with limb deformities; many stillbirths
Led to introduction of strict drug-testing regulations worldwide
Drugs must now be tested for: safety, efficacy, and correct dosage
Thalidomide now used (strictly) for leprosy and some cancers — not in pregnancy

keyTerms.def: A drug prescribed in the 1950s–60s for morning sickness that caused severe birth defects — its failure led to modern drug testing regulations
How well a drug works — whether it actually treats the condition it is intended for
The correct amount of a drug needed to be effective without causing harm
How poisonous or harmful a substance is to living cells or the body

keyPoints.memTip: Thalidomide = the reason drug testing exists. In the exam, if asked why drugs need to be tested, thalidomide is your go-to example. Always state: what it was for, what went wrong, and what changed. Three facts, three marks.

keyPoints.heading: Preclinical testing — before the first human volunteer
keyPoints.content: Before any new drug reaches a human, it goes through preclinical testing. This happens in two stages. First, laboratory testing: the drug is tested on cell cultures (growing cells in a dish) and using computer models that simulate how the drug interacts with human biology. These tests are fast and cheap, and can screen out drugs that are obviously toxic or inactive early on. Second, animal testing: if the lab data looks promising, the drug is tested in animals. This reveals how the drug behaves in a living body — how it is absorbed, how it is broken down, and whether it causes unexpected harm. Scientists test for toxicity (is it poisonous?), efficacy (does it work in a living organism?), and dosage (what amount is effective and what amount is harmful?). Only drugs that pass preclinical testing move forward to human trials.
writeBullets: Preclinical stage 1: cell cultures and computer models — fast, cheap screening
Preclinical stage 2: animal testing — how drug behaves in a living body
Three things tested: toxicity, efficacy, dosage
Drugs that fail at preclinical stage never reach human trials
Computer models used to reduce the number of animals needed

keyTerms.def: Drug testing before human trials — uses cell cultures, computer models and animals to check safety and effectiveness
Growing cells in a controlled laboratory environment to test how they respond to a drug
A simulation used to predict how a drug will interact with the human body, reducing the need for animal tests

keyPoints.memTip: Preclinical = PRE-clinical (before people). The order is cells → computers → animals → humans. Each stage is more complex and closer to real human biology than the last. If you mix up the order in an exam, that's a common mistake — practise saying it aloud: lab first, animals second, humans third.

keyPoints.heading: Clinical trials — testing in humans
keyPoints.content: Once a drug passes preclinical tests, it enters clinical trials — testing in humans. Clinical trials happen in phases: Phase 1: a small group of healthy volunteers. The focus is on safety — is the drug safe for humans? What side effects occur? What dose can a person tolerate? Phase 2: a small group of patients who have the condition the drug is meant to treat. The focus is on efficacy — does it actually work? Is the dose right? Phase 3: a large group of patients. This is where the double-blind, placebo-controlled trial is used (covered in the next section). The aim is to confirm efficacy and safety across a large, diverse group. Phase 4: after the drug is approved and on the market, post-approval monitoring continues. Rare side effects that only appear in very large populations are detected at this stage.
writeBullets: Phase 1: small group, healthy volunteers, tests SAFETY
Phase 2: small group, patients with the disease, tests EFFICACY
Phase 3: large group, patients, double-blind placebo-controlled trial
Phase 4: post-approval monitoring — detects rare or long-term side effects
Each phase must pass before moving to the next — the process filters out unsafe or ineffective drugs

keyTerms.def: A study that tests a drug or treatment in human volunteers to check safety and effectiveness
First human trial — small number of healthy volunteers, testing mainly for safety and side effects
Large-scale human trial using a double-blind placebo-controlled design to confirm both safety and efficacy
Ongoing surveillance of a drug after it reaches the market, to detect rare or long-term side effects (Phase 4)

keyPoints.memTip: Phases in order: 1 = Safety (healthy people); 2 = Efficacy (sick people, small); 3 = Confirm everything (large, double-blind); 4 = After launch (ongoing). A useful check: each phase is bigger than the last. If you can remember '1 healthy, 2 patients small, 3 patients large', you have the skeleton for a 4-mark clinical trial question.

keyPoints.heading: Double-blind placebo trials — removing the human bias
keyPoints.content: The most rigorous form of clinical trial is the double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Here is why each element matters. A placebo is a treatment that looks identical to the real drug but contains no active ingredient — it might be a sugar pill or a saline injection. It's given to the control group. This controls for the placebo effect: some patients feel better simply because they believe they're being treated. If both groups improve, the drug effect has to be shown to be greater than placebo. Double-blind means neither the patient nor the doctor knows who is receiving the drug or the placebo during the trial. If the patient doesn't know, they can't convince themselves the drug is working. If the doctor doesn't know, they can't unconsciously interpret results more favourably for patients they know are on the real drug. Both sources of bias are eliminated at once. Results are only revealed at the end of the trial. This design is considered the gold standard in clinical research — it gives the most reliable, unbiased data.
writeBullets: Placebo: looks identical to the drug but has no active ingredient — controls for the placebo effect
Placebo effect: patients feel better just because they believe they're being treated
Double-blind: neither patient nor doctor knows who gets drug or placebo
Removes patient bias (expectation) AND doctor bias (interpretation)
Results are revealed only at the end — ensures objective data collection
Double-blind placebo trial = gold standard in clinical research

keyTerms.def: An inactive treatment (e.g. sugar pill) given to the control group in a trial — used to separate the real drug effect from the placebo effect
Improvement in a patient's condition caused by the belief that they are receiving treatment, not by the treatment itself
A trial where neither the patient nor the doctor knows who is receiving the drug or placebo — eliminates both types of bias
The group in an experiment that receives the placebo (or no treatment) — used for comparison with the treatment group
A systematic distortion in results caused by expectation or prior knowledge — removed by double-blinding

keyPoints.memTip: Double = TWO people don't know: the PATIENT and the DOCTOR. If only one person doesn't know, it's single-blind — and that's not good enough, because the doctor might still influence the results. For the exam, practise explaining WHY the doctor being blinded matters, not just the patient.

1.question: Why did the thalidomide disaster lead to a complete overhaul of drug-testing regulations?
options: Thalidomide was found to cause addiction in the patients who took it
The drug had not been tested for its effects during pregnancy, leading to severe birth defects in thousands of children
Thalidomide was found to be completely ineffective at treating morning sickness
The drug caused heart failure in elderly patients who were prescribed it
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — thalidomide hadn't been tested on pregnant animals. The result was devastating birth defects in over 10,000 babies. It made clear that drugs must be tested for all possible effects before approval.
1.wrongFeedback: The thalidomide disaster happened because the drug was never tested on pregnant animals. It was prescribed to pregnant women for morning sickness, and caused severe limb deformities in more than 10,000 babies. The lesson was that testing must cover all relevant situations before approval.

3.question: In a double-blind placebo-controlled trial, why is it important that the doctor also doesn't know which patients are receiving the drug?
options: So the doctor cannot accidentally give the wrong dose to patients
To prevent the doctor from unconsciously interpreting or recording results more favourably for patients they know are on the real drug
Because doctors are not allowed to know which drug is being tested for legal reasons
So the placebo and drug are the same cost to administer
3.correctFeedback: Correct — if the doctor knows who is on the real drug, they might unconsciously record improvements more readily for those patients, biasing the results. Blinding the doctor eliminates that source of error.
3.wrongFeedback: The reason is bias. If a doctor knows a patient is on the real drug, they may unconsciously interpret ambiguous results more positively for that patient. Double-blind means neither patient nor doctor knows — removing both sources of bias for the most reliable data.

commonMistakes: Saying the placebo is 'fake' medicine — describe it precisely as an inactive treatment that looks identical to the real drug, used to isolate the placebo effect.
Thinking double-blind means two groups — it means two types of people (patient and doctor) who don't know the allocation. The number of groups is separate from the blinding.
Confusing Phase 1 and Phase 2 — Phase 1 uses healthy volunteers (safety), Phase 2 uses patients (efficacy). Easy to swap under pressure; practise saying them in order.
Stating thalidomide was only harmful and never used again — AQA includes the fact that it is now used for leprosy and some cancers under strict conditions. Show you know this.
Forgetting to mention the placebo effect when explaining why a placebo is needed — the examiner wants you to name it, not just say 'to compare results'.
examTips: When explaining a double-blind trial, always name BOTH people who are kept in the dark (patient AND doctor) and explain WHY each one matters — you can lose a mark for only naming one.

For a 'describe the stages of drug testing' question, give the stages in order: preclinical (cells/computers, then animals) → clinical Phase 1 (healthy, safety) → Phase 2 (patients, efficacy) → Phase 3 (large, double-blind) → Phase 4 (post-market). Each stage is worth a mark.

AQA sometimes asks you to evaluate whether a trial design is valid — check if it's double-blind, if there's a placebo, if the sample is large enough, and if the right population was tested. Use those criteria to judge the design.

revisionCardBullets: Drugs must be tested for: safety, efficacy, and correct dosage — before any human takes them
Thalidomide: morning sickness drug → limb deformities in 10,000+ babies → overhaul of testing regulations
Thalidomide now used for leprosy and some cancers under strict controls — never in pregnancy
Preclinical: cell cultures + computer models (stage 1) → animal tests (stage 2)
Phase 1 clinical trial: healthy volunteers, small group — tests SAFETY
Phase 2 clinical trial: patients, small group — tests EFFICACY
Phase 3 clinical trial: patients, large group — double-blind, placebo-controlled
Phase 4: post-approval monitoring — detects rare or long-term side effects
Placebo: inactive treatment that looks like the real drug — controls for placebo effect
